title: Authentication vs Authorization
description: Understand the difference between these two security concepts.

slides.title: Authentication – Who Are You? 🆔
slides.content:
# Authentication

Authentication is the process of verifying **who** a user is. It answers the question: "Are you who you claim to be?"

Common methods:
- Username/password
- Biometrics (fingerprint, face)
- OAuth (Google, Facebook login)
- API keys
- Tokens (JWT)

After successful authentication, the system knows the user's identity.

slides.title: Authorization – What Can You Do? 🔑
slides.content:
# Authorization

Authorization determines **what** an authenticated user is allowed to do. It answers: "Do you have permission to access this resource?"

Examples:
- A regular user can view their own profile but not edit others'.
- An admin can delete any post.
- A guest can only see public content.

Authorization usually happens after authentication, using roles, permissions, or policies.

slides.title: Analogy: Airport Security ✈️
slides.content:
## Analogy

- **Authentication**: Showing your passport at the check-in counter to prove your identity.
- **Authorization**: After boarding, your ticket determines whether you sit in economy, business, or first class.

You need both: first prove who you are, then the system checks what you're allowed to do.

quizData.question: Which process determines what resources a user can access?
quizData.explanation: Authorization controls access after identity is verified.
